tagline: Switch to the Murmur keyboard. Speak. It types.
description: Native iOS Murmur: a SwiftUI app plus a custom keyboard extension, sharing the desktop app's formatter brains through spec files in shared/. Apple forbids microphone access inside keyboard extensions, so dictation uses the containing-app bounce that Wispr Flow proved: the keyboard's mic key opens the Murmur app, the app records and transcribes through the same Groq pipeline as desktop, and the finished text returns to the keyboard through the App Group container. Action Button and Siri Shortcut triggers reduce the keyboard-switching friction.

stack.keyboard: Custom keyboard extension (UIInputViewController), dictation-first layout, not a full QWERTY in v1
stack.transcription: Groq audio/transcriptions (whisper-large-v3-turbo) or any OpenAI-compatible base URL, same as desktop
stack.formatting: Groq chat/completions driven by shared/format-spec.json, fail-open, same as desktop
stack.sharing: App Group container for keyboard-app data, murmur:// URL scheme for the bounce
stack.verification: xcodebuild test on the iOS Simulator is the smoke equivalent; unit tests mirror the desktop smoke checks with shared test vectors

conventions.definitionOfDone: A story passes only when every acceptance criterion is verified. Criteria needing a physical iPhone, a live microphone, a real API key, or App Store Connect are verified manually by Labroi and noted in notes.
conventions.process: Work stories in priority order. Keep xcodebuild test green, and keep the desktop's npm run smoke green whenever shared/ or src/ is touched. After completing a story, set passes=true, record how it was verified in notes, run npm run roadmap, and commit. git pull --rebase before every commit: parallel sessions run on other machines.

hardConstraints: The keyboard extension never touches the microphone. Apple forbids it; all audio lives in the containing app.
The API key lives in the iOS Keychain, is shared to no extension, and never appears in the repo, UserDefaults, or logs.
Audio goes only to the configured API; transcripts and history stay on device; the App Group carries only what the keyboard needs.
The formatter fails open: a dictation is never lost because the cleanup LLM errored or chatted.
No binary assets in the repo: iOS icons are generated by script into the asset catalog.
The keyboard extension stays lean: no networking libraries, no audio frameworks, well under the extension memory ceiling.
Night studio design system: ink surfaces, warm text, amber reserved for live states, red for record and errors.
shared/format-spec.json is the single source of truth for formatter rules on every platform.

stories.title: Shared formatter spec
stories.description: As a maintainer, the formatter rules (levels, styles, structure, numbers, chat-guard tells) live in shared/format-spec.json, and both the desktop app and iOS build their prompts from it, so tuning once ships everywhere.
acceptanceCriteria: shared/format-spec.json holds the level rules, style rules, structure rules, number rules, base rules, and chat-guard tell phrases currently hardcoded in src/main/transcribe.js

Desktop transcribe.js composes its prompt from the spec file with behavior byte-identical to before (existing formatPrompt, structurePrompt, numberPrompt, formatChatGuard smoke checks unchanged and green)
A new required desktop smoke check (specDriven) proves the prompt changes when the spec file changes, so the wiring cannot silently regress to constants
shared/test-vectors.json captures the correction, expansion, and chat-guard cases the desktop smoke checks use, so iOS unit tests consume the identical vectors
npm run smoke green on desktop after the refactor
stories.notes: Verified 2026-07-23. shared/format-spec.json now holds header, rules label, spoken commands, footer, dictionary and corrections rule templates, vocabulary prompt, all five levels, structure rules, both styles, number rules, chat-guard tells plus thresholds, and the silence no_speech_prob threshold. transcribe.js composes from the spec (buildFormatPrompt, guardFormatOutput, extractTranscript take an optional spec arg defaulting to the file). Byte-identical proven by snapshotting all 72 level x style x numbers prompt combos before the refactor and diffing after: zero mismatches. specDriven required check proves the default prompt contains strings read from the file on disk and that a mutated spec changes the output, so a regression to constants fails smoke. shared/test-vectors.json carries the correction, expansion, chat-guard, and silence-segment cases; new required sharedVectors check runs every vector through the real desktop implementations. shared/**/* added to builder files so packaged apps ship the spec. npm run smoke green: all 26 required checks true including specDriven and sharedVectors, formatPrompt, structurePrompt, numberPrompt, formatChatGuard untouched and green.

stories.title: Xcode project, app shell, and project law
stories.description: As a developer, ios/ contains a working Xcode project: the Murmur SwiftUI app and the keyboard extension target, an App Group linking them, generated icons, and a unit test target that is the smoke equivalent.
acceptanceCriteria: ios/ Xcode project builds the app and keyboard extension targets cleanly via xcodebuild from a fresh clone
App target and keyboard target share an App Group; bundle identifiers are consistent (com.labroi.murmur.ios and a keyboard child id)
xcodebuild test runs a unit test target on the iOS Simulator and passes; this command is documented in CLAUDE.md as the iOS verification gate
App icons and the keyboard icon are generated by a script (no binary assets committed); the asset catalog consumes the generated files, and generation is documented
Night studio design tokens (ink, panel, warm text, amber, red, mono) defined once in Swift and used by the app shell
The murmur:// URL scheme is registered and routes into the app
stories.notes: Verified 2026-07-23 on Xcode 26.6, iOS 26.5 simulator. Hand-authored pbxproj (objectVersion 77, synchronized folder groups) with three targets: Murmur app, MurmurKeyboard extension, MurmurTests hosted unit tests. xcodebuild -scheme Murmur -destination iPhone 17 build succeeds with the appex embedded and validated; xcodebuild test runs 10 tests, all pass (spec and vector bundles, defaults, thresholds, murmur:// route parsing, App Group container resolution). Gate documented in CLAUDE.md, device name updated to a simulator that exists (iPhone 16 does not ship with Xcode 26). Bundle ids com.labroi.murmur.ios, .keyboard, .tests; both entitlements files carry group.com.labroi.murmur.ios and the hosted test resolves the container. scripts/gen-ios-icons.js reuses the desktop PNG writer: opaque RGB 1024 app icon (App Store rejects alpha) plus KeyboardGlyph 1x/2x/3x, wired into postinstall and npm run icons:ios, PNGs gitignored, generation documented in CLAUDE.md. Night studio tokens defined once in ios/Shared/DesignTokens.swift, compiled into both app and keyboard targets, used by the shell and keyboard placeholder. Live-verified in the simulator: app installed, launched, xcrun simctl openurl murmur://dictate?session=smoke1 raised the OS Open in Murmur confirmation (scheme registered) and after accepting, the shell shows BOUNCE RECEIVED SMOKE1 (routed through onOpenURL, screenshot verified). Desktop npm run smoke green after the gen-icons.js helper export refactor.

stories.title: Transcription and formatting pipeline in Swift
stories.description: As a user, my speech recorded by the iOS app goes through the same pipeline as desktop: Whisper transcription with silence-hallucination guards, corrections, fail-open formatting from the shared spec, then expansions, entirely in the app process.
acceptanceCriteria: AVAudioSession records mono compressed audio suitable for the transcriptions endpoint, with a live level meter exposed for UI
Transcription posts to {baseUrl}/audio/transcriptions with verbose_json and drops segments with no_speech_prob > 0.85, matching desktop

The formatter prompt is composed from shared/format-spec.json (style, level, numbers, structure) and the chat guard rejects conversational replies using the shared tells, failing open to the raw transcript
Corrections apply case-insensitive whole-word, and expansions apply last so values never reach any API, both matching desktop semantics
Unit tests cover the pipeline against shared/test-vectors.json plus canned Whisper JSON fixtures (silence segments, chatty formatter replies), all green in xcodebuild test
Network errors surface as readable messages (bad key, rate limit, offline, timeout), never silent failures
stories.notes: Built 2026-07-23, all logic unit-verified, xcodebuild test green (6 suites). Swift port in ios/Murmur/Pipeline/: FormatSpec (Codable face of the shared spec), Formatter (prompt composition proven byte-identical to desktop via full prompt strings in shared/test-vectors.json formatPrompt section, generated by the desktop implementation and re-proven every desktop smoke run; chat guard uses UTF-16 lengths to match JS semantics), Corrections and Expansions (JS \w boundary class [A-Za-z0-9_] exactly), Transcriber (multipart verbose_json request, segment filter from spec threshold, readable errors for 401, 429, timeout, offline, fail-open smartFormat), Pipeline (desktop handleAudio order: transcribe, silence refusal, corrections, format, expansions dead last), Recorder (AAC mono 16 kHz m4a, 30 Hz level meter, format pinned by test). Privacy proven by test: expansion values appear in no request body. Remaining before passes: live capture and a real transcription round trip need Labroi's mic and API key; both happen inside the US-105 live loop, flip this then.

stories.title: API key, settings, and onboarding
stories.description: As a new user, the app walks me through the three things it needs: a free Groq key, microphone permission, and adding the Murmur keyboard, and my settings mirror the desktop's semantics.
acceptanceCriteria: API key stored in the Keychain with a Test connection button hitting {baseUrl}/models; key never written to UserDefaults or the App Group
Settings expose model, formatting style, level, numbers, language, and Base URL with the same options and defaults as desktop
Onboarding: get key (link to console.groq.com), paste and test, grant microphone, then an add-the-keyboard walkthrough with the exact Settings path and an explanation of why Full Access is needed
Onboarding never shows again after completion; every screen usable with VoiceOver labels
Settings persist across relaunches; unit tests cover settings round-trip and key storage isolation
stories.notes: Built 2026-07-23, xcodebuild test green (7 suites). Keychain.swift stores the key as a this-device-only generic password, no access group; SettingsStore mirrors desktop defaults and option lists exactly (unit tests pin both against src/main/settings.js and the settings renderer lists). Key isolation proven by test: key set through the store lands in the Keychain, reads back in a fresh instance, and appears nowhere in standard defaults, the test suite, or the App Group suite; clearing the key removes the Keychain item. Settings round-trip across instances tested (a second store over the same defaults is a relaunch). SettingsView exposes model, language, smart formatting, style, level, numbers, formatting model, Base URL with Test connection hitting {baseUrl}/models. Onboarding four steps: Groq key with console.groq.com link and paste-and-test, microphone grant, add-the-keyboard walkthrough (Settings, Murmur, Keyboards path) with the honest Full Access explanation, finish sets onboarded which never resets (round-trip tested). VoiceOver labels on every interactive element. Step 1 screenshot-verified in the simulator: night studio, amber reserved for actions. Remaining for Labroi: test connection with his real key and a VoiceOver spot check on device; flip passes then.

stories.title: In-app dictation
stories.description: As a user, I can dictate inside the Murmur app itself: hold the big amber-ringed button, speak, release, and get clean text with a live waveform while I talk.
acceptanceCriteria: Hold-to-talk button records while held and stops on release; a tap toggles for long takes
Live waveform driven by real mic amplitude; processing and inserted states visually distinct; amber only while live
Result appears in a card with Copy and Share actions and lands in local history
Errors (silence, network, key) show readable messages in the same card
History stores the last 200 dictations on device with copy and delete, and can be turned off
Verified live by Labroi on his iPhone: dictate, copy, paste into another app
stories.notes: Built 2026-07-23, xcodebuild test green (8 suites). DictationController: hold records while held, release under 0.35s toggles a long take (decision pinned by unit test), press during a toggled take stops it; phases idle, recording, processing, done, error. DictationView: 36-bar waveform driven by the recorder's 30 Hz level meter, amber strictly while recording, result card with Copy and ShareLink, error card in red with dismiss, history list with per-item copy and delete. HistoryStore: last 200 on device in Application Support JSON, newest first, cap and delete and relaunch persistence unit-tested; history toggle added to Settings. Live-verified in the simulator with a synthesized 2s hold: LISTENING state screenshot shows amber ring, amber mark, amber status while recording real (silent) mic input; release screenshot shows recording stopped, amber gone, and the readable red error card No API key yet with dismiss. Remaining for Labroi on his iPhone: real dictation with his Groq key, copy, paste into another app; flip passes then.

stories.title: Keyboard extension
stories.description: As a user, I switch to the Murmur keyboard in any app and see a dictation-first keyboard: a big mic key plus space, delete, return, and globe, in the night studio look. Typing letters is what the system keyboard is for; Murmur's keyboard is for speaking.
acceptanceCriteria: Keyboard extension appears in iOS keyboard settings after install and renders the dictation-first layout in any host app
Mic key opens the containing app through the URL scheme with a session token; space, delete, return, and globe work as expected
Without Full Access the keyboard shows a clear inline explanation and a path to enable it instead of failing silently
The keyboard reads only the App Group result store; it contains no networking or audio code (verified by review and noted)
Memory footprint measured in Instruments stays well under the extension ceiling; no termination during a normal session
Number pad, email, and decimal fields hand off to the system keyboard rather than presenting a broken layout

stories.title: The bounce: record, return, insert
stories.description: As a user, tapping the keyboard's mic key bounces me to Murmur, recording starts instantly, I speak, and when I come back to the app I was in, the transcribed text inserts at my cursor.
acceptanceCriteria: The app launched via the mic-key URL starts recording immediately, no taps, with the waveform and a stop button on screen
Recording auto-stops on tap or after a configurable max duration; the transcript is written to the App Group store keyed by the session token
Returning to the host app, the keyboard detects the finished session and inserts the text at the cursor exactly once (token consumed, no double inserts, stale sessions expire)
Where iOS allows automatic return, use it; where it requires a manual swipe back (iOS 26.4 behavior), a clear swipe-back hint shows after the take completes
The full loop is under 10 seconds for a one-sentence dictation on device
Failure paths (silence, network error, user abandons the bounce) leave a readable state in the keyboard, never a hang or a silent nothing
Verified live by Labroi: dictate into Messages and into Notes via the Murmur keyboard on his iPhone

stories.title: Action Button and Siri Shortcut dictation
stories.description: As a user, I can start a Murmur dictation without touching the keyboard: from the iPhone Action Button or a Siri Shortcut, speak, and the transcript lands on my clipboard ready to paste anywhere.
acceptanceCriteria: A Dictate with Murmur App Intent appears in Shortcuts and is assignable to the Action Button
Invoking it opens recording instantly; the finished transcript is copied to the clipboard and shown in a confirmation
The intent returns the transcript text so Shortcuts can chain it into other actions
Works from the lock screen path Apple allows (app opens to record); limitation documented honestly if recording cannot start without unlock
Verified live by Labroi from the Action Button on his iPhone

stories.title: Dictionary, corrections, and expansions on iOS
stories.description: As a user, my vocabulary works on my phone like on my desktop: dictionary terms hint the transcriber and formatter, fixing a transcript teaches a correction, and expansions insert literal values that never leave the device.
acceptanceCriteria: Dictionary terms managed in the app, fed to the Whisper prompt and the formatter exactly as desktop does
Editing a history item diffs and learns correction pairs with the same promote-on-second-fix rule, sharing the desktop's diff semantics via shared test vectors
Expansions managed in the app and applied post-formatter; values never appear in any request body (unit test proves it)
All three behaviors covered in xcodebuild test using shared/test-vectors.json
Verified live by Labroi with one taught correction reapplying on a later dictation

stories.title: TestFlight on Labroi's iPhone
stories.description: As the owner, I install Murmur from TestFlight on my iPhone, and the repo documents exactly how a release gets there, so shipping updates is a repeatable loop instead of an adventure.
acceptanceCriteria: Archive and upload path documented step by step (signing with the Developer team, App Store Connect app record, TestFlight processing), with every step that needs Labroi's hands called out explicitly
Version and build numbers managed sanely (one place to bump)
A TestFlight build installs and runs on Labroi's physical iPhone with the keyboard enabled and the bounce working end to end
Privacy nutrition labels and the App Group entitlements pass App Store Connect validation
README gains an iOS section: what it is, the keyboard-detour reality, setup steps, and the TestFlight path
